items.prompt_md: In the greedy scan for counting non-overlapping intervals, why sort by **end** rather than by **start**?
options: Keeping the interval with the earliest end leaves the most room for future intervals, which is what the count depends on
Sorting by end avoids needing a comparison function at all
Sorting by end guarantees the intervals are already merged
Sorting by end makes the list shorter before scanning
items.answer: Keeping the interval with the earliest end leaves the most room for future intervals, which is what the count depends on
items.explanation_md: A long interval that starts early but ends late can block more future intervals than a short one that ends sooner. Sorting by end and always keeping the one that finishes earliest is the greedy choice that never blocks more than necessary.

items.prompt_md: While sweeping end-sorted intervals and holding `prev_end`, what test decides whether the current interval is compatible with (doesn't overlap) the last kept interval?
options: The current interval's start is at or after `prev_end`
The current interval's end is at or after `prev_end`
The current interval's start is strictly less than `prev_end`
The current interval is shorter than the last kept interval
items.answer: The current interval's start is at or after `prev_end`
items.explanation_md: Since the list is sorted by end, `prev_end` is always the earliest possible finish among kept intervals so far; anything starting at or after it doesn't overlap what's already been kept.

items.prompt_md: When an interval IS compatible (its start is at or after `prev_end`), what should the scan do?
options: Keep it and update `prev_end` to this interval's end
Keep it but leave `prev_end` unchanged
Discard it since it might overlap a later interval
Merge it into the previous interval's range
items.answer: Keep it and update `prev_end` to this interval's end
items.explanation_md: Keeping the interval advances the frontier: `prev_end` must reflect the newly kept interval's end so the next compatibility check is correct.

items.prompt_md: When an interval is NOT compatible with `prev_end` (it overlaps the last kept interval), what happens to `prev_end`?
options: It stays the same — the overlapping interval is dropped, not kept
It updates to the overlapping interval's end, since that's the newest information
It resets to negative infinity
It updates to the overlapping interval's start
items.answer: It stays the same — the overlapping interval is dropped, not kept
items.explanation_md: The dropped interval never becomes the "last kept" interval, so the frontier `prev_end` must not move — otherwise a later interval could wrongly be judged compatible against an interval that was never actually retained.

items.prompt_md: How does the number of intervals to REMOVE relate to the number of intervals KEPT during the scan?
options: removed = total count − kept count
removed = kept count, since every kept interval implies one removal
removed is always kept count − 1
removed has no fixed relationship to kept count
items.answer: removed = total count − kept count
items.explanation_md: The scan classifies every interval as either kept (compatible) or removed (overlapping), so the two counts always partition the full list.

items.prompt_md: A buggy implementation uses `start > prev_end` (strictly greater) instead of `start >= prev_end` as the compatibility test. What goes wrong?
options: Intervals that merely touch at a shared endpoint get wrongly treated as overlapping and removed
Nothing goes wrong — touching endpoints never occur in valid input
It causes the scan to keep every interval regardless of overlap
It only affects the very first interval in the list
items.answer: Intervals that merely touch at a shared endpoint get wrongly treated as overlapping and removed
items.explanation_md: For counting/selecting, touching endpoints (e.g. `[1,5]` then `[5,8]`) are compatible — they don't share an interior point — so the test must allow equality, not exclude it.

items.prompt_md: This lesson's greedy scan and the merge-intervals lesson both sort the input, but for different reasons. What's the key difference?
options: This lesson sorts by end to maximize a non-overlapping SUBSET; merge-intervals sorts by start to fuse everything into a covering range
They sort by the same key and solve the same problem, just phrased differently
This lesson doesn't need a sort at all, unlike merge-intervals
Merge-intervals sorts by end too, but this lesson sorts by length
items.answer: This lesson sorts by end to maximize a non-overlapping SUBSET; merge-intervals sorts by start to fuse everything into a covering range
items.explanation_md: The two lessons solve opposite goals — covering everything (merge, sort by start) vs. selecting the largest compatible subset (count/select, sort by end) — so they need different preprocessing keys.

items.prompt_md: Why is trying every subset of intervals to find the largest non-overlapping one a bad approach?
options: It costs O(2^n), since every subset must be checked for internal overlaps
It costs O(n^2) and is only slightly worse than the greedy scan
It's actually the same complexity as the greedy scan, just less readable
It fails to find the correct answer in most cases
items.answer: It costs O(2^n), since every subset must be checked for internal overlaps
items.explanation_md: There are 2^n subsets of n intervals; checking each for pairwise non-overlap is exponential, versus the greedy scan's O(n log n).

items.prompt_md: A variant problem asks for the number of meeting rooms needed at every instant, not just the size of the largest non-overlapping set. Why doesn't this lesson's single-pass greedy scan directly answer that?
options: That variant needs a LIVE, constantly-updated concurrent count as time progresses, not a one-time maximum independent set
It's exactly the same problem and the greedy scan already gives the live count
The greedy scan only works for two intervals at a time
Meeting rooms is a smaller problem than counting overlaps, so it never needs a different approach
items.answer: That variant needs a LIVE, constantly-updated concurrent count as time progresses, not a one-time maximum independent set
items.explanation_md: "How many are in progress at once, as things start and end" is a running concurrent count — that calls for a min-heap of end times (or separate sorted start/end sweeps), a different tool from this lesson's one-shot greedy selection.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the sort-by-end greedy scan over n intervals?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
items.answer: O(n log n)
items.explanation_md: The scan that keeps or drops each interval is a single O(n) pass, but sorting the intervals by end dominates at O(n log n), which is the overall bound — far better than the O(2^n) brute-force subset search.

items.prompt_md: What is the auxiliary space complexity of the greedy scan, ignoring the space used by the sort itself?
options: O(1) extra, since only `prev_end` and a running removed-count are tracked
O(n), because every interval must be stored in a separate visited set
O(n log n), matching the sort's footprint
O(n^2), because every pair of intervals is compared
items.answer: O(1) extra, since only `prev_end` and a running removed-count are tracked
items.explanation_md: Beyond whatever the sort allocates internally, the scan itself only needs two running scalars.

items.prompt_md: You're given a list of intervals and must find the maximum number of them that can coexist without any two overlapping.
options: Sort by end time and greedily keep every interval whose start is at or after the last kept interval's end
Sort by start time and merge each interval into the last one whenever it overlaps
Try every subset of intervals and check each one for overlaps
Build a prefix-sum array over interval endpoints and scan for the maximum
items.answer: Sort by end time and greedily keep every interval whose start is at or after the last kept interval's end
items.explanation_md: "Maximum non-overlapping subset" is the sort-by-end greedy selection — the earliest-ending compatible choice never blocks more future intervals than any alternative.

items.prompt_md: Given a set of scheduled jobs with start/end times, find the minimum number of jobs you'd need to cancel so that no two remaining jobs overlap.
options: Sort by end time and greedily keep every interval whose start is at or after the last kept interval's end
Sort by start time and merge overlapping ranges into one
Min-heap of end times, popping whenever a new job starts
Brute-force all pairs of jobs
items.answer: Sort by end time and greedily keep every interval whose start is at or after the last kept interval's end
items.explanation_md: "Minimum removals so nothing overlaps" is the complement of "maximum kept without overlap" — the same sort-by-end greedy scan answers both, since removed = total − kept.

items.prompt_md: You need to know, at every single moment of the day, exactly how many meetings are simultaneously in progress, as each one starts and ends.
options: Sort by end time and greedily keep compatible intervals
Min-heap of end times
Brute-force all pairs
Hash map
items.answer: Min-heap of end times
items.explanation_md: Trap: this asks for a LIVE, constantly-updated concurrent count, not a one-time maximum non-overlapping subset — that calls for a min-heap of end times (or an event sweep), not this lesson's single-pass greedy count.

items.prompt_md: You must assign the minimum number of meeting rooms so that no two meetings sharing a room ever overlap.
options: Sort by end time and greedily keep compatible intervals
Min-heap of end times, tracking how many rooms are occupied at once
Brute-force all pairs
Sort by start and merge into one covering range
items.answer: Min-heap of end times, tracking how many rooms are occupied at once
items.explanation_md: Trap: minimum simultaneous rooms is a live concurrency count (pop the heap's earliest end whenever a new meeting starts before it frees up), not a maximum-independent-set count.

items.prompt_md: From a long list of overlapping TV show air-time slots, pick the largest possible number of slots you could watch live without any two overlapping.
options: Sort by end time and greedily keep compatible intervals
Sort by start time and merge overlapping ranges
Brute-force all subsets
Two-pointer scan from both ends
items.answer: Sort by end time and greedily keep compatible intervals
items.explanation_md: "Largest number watchable without overlap" is exactly the maximum non-overlapping subset goal — sort by end, greedily keep whatever is compatible.

items.prompt_md: You're given two people's already-sorted lists of free time slots and must find every time range where both are free at once.
options: Walk both lists with two pointers, advancing whichever interval ends first
Sort by end time and greedily keep compatible intervals
Brute-force all pairs across the two lists
Min-heap of end times
items.answer: Walk both lists with two pointers, advancing whichever interval ends first
items.explanation_md: Trap: reconciling two already-sorted lists against each other for pairwise overlaps is a two-pointer merge, not this lesson's single-list greedy count.

items.prompt_md: Given a pile of overlapping event intervals, you need the fewest number of blocks that still cover every event's full time span.
options: Sort by start time and merge overlapping ranges into a covering set
Sort by end time and greedily keep compatible intervals
Min-heap of end times
Hash map
items.answer: Sort by start time and merge overlapping ranges into a covering set
items.explanation_md: Trap: "cover every span with the fewest blocks" is the merge-intervals goal (sort by start, fuse touching/crossing ranges) — the opposite preprocessing key from counting a maximum non-overlapping subset.

items.prompt_md: You have a set of exam time-slots and want to schedule the maximum number of exams in one room, with no two ever overlapping.
options: Sort by end time and greedily keep compatible intervals
Sort by start time and merge into a covering range
Brute-force all pairs
Recursion over every subset
items.answer: Sort by end time and greedily keep compatible intervals
items.explanation_md: "Maximum number schedulable in one room, none overlapping" is the classic interval-scheduling-maximization greedy — sort by end and keep the earliest-ending compatible choice each time.

items.prompt_md: You have a stream of arriving and departing airplane time slots at a single runway and must count, at each instant, how many planes are actively using it.
options: Sort by end time and greedily keep compatible intervals
Min-heap of end times, or a start/end event sweep
Brute-force all pairs
Sort by start and merge into a covering range
items.answer: Min-heap of end times, or a start/end event sweep
items.explanation_md: Trap: an instant-by-instant concurrency count needs a live tracker (heap or event sweep) that updates as intervals start and end — not this lesson's one-shot maximum-subset count.

items.prompt_md: You're given a list of contiguous work shifts with overlaps and need to know the maximum number of them a single worker could complete without ever double-booking.
options: Sort by end time and greedily keep compatible intervals
Sort by start time and merge overlapping ranges
Try every subset of shifts and check for internal overlaps
Hash map of shift start times
items.answer: Sort by end time and greedily keep compatible intervals
items.explanation_md: "Maximum a single worker could complete without double-booking" is the maximum non-overlapping subset — sort by end and greedily keep whatever's compatible with the last kept shift.
